summary: The author developed a 5th place solution for a partially observable multi-agent reinforcement learning competition using an xLSTM-based recurrent architecture combined with a Transformer encoder. The approach separates actor and critic networks, with the critic having access to ground-truth information while the actor relies on encoded observations. Training followed a multi-phase curriculum starting with a smaller model, followed by knowledge distillation to a larger model, and finally extensive PPO training with shaped rewards. Self-play against a buffer of past checkpoints was used to stabilize learning, and the entire pipeline was implemented in JAX for efficiency.
extensive_summary:
## Architecture & Input Encoding
The solution centers on an xLSTM-based recurrent agent designed for a partially observable environment. Observations are split into global features (15-value vector for actor, 18-value for critic), map features (24x24x11 for actor, 24x24x15 for critic), and unit features (32 5-value vectors + positions for both friendly and opponent units). Positional encodings (x, y coordinates) are added to the map, and all features are flipped so the player always appears in the top-left corner. Relic fragment locations are tracked via a history of unit movements and point gains, with probabilities iteratively updated.

## Model Design
The encoder processes map features through a ConvNeXt (3 blocks without resolution reduction to extract unit-location features, then compressed to a 128-dim vector), while friendly/opponent units and global state pass through shared MLPs with GELU and LayerNorm. These tokens (map, recurrent, 32 unit tokens, global) are fed into a Transformer meta-encoder with self-attention and gated MLPs. The core recurrent component is an xLSTM (combining mLSTM for memory and sLSTM layers), implemented in pure JAX. The actor head uses a transformer decoder with cross-attention over unit embeddings and map features to predict action types and sap locations, while the critic head is a simpler MLP with spectral norm. Both networks contain approximately 2M parameters.

## Training Strategy
Training followed a phased curriculum. A smaller ~500K parameter model was trained first on a 16x16 map with shaped rewards, then scaled to a 24x24 map with shaped rewards, and finally sparse rewards. To scale up, a larger model was trained via knowledge distillation from the small model's logits (using cross-entropy loss) for 100M steps, followed by ~800M steps of PPO with shaped rewards. Self-play was implemented by vmap-ing over 128 past checkpoints (25% of games) and the latest checkpoint (75% of games), keeping all weights on GPU. The entire pipeline was built in JAX, leveraging JIT compilation and bfloat16 for efficiency.
competition_flow: Raw game observations (global features, map tiles, unit states) are encoded via ConvNeXt and MLPs, passed through a Transformer and xLSTM recurrent core, and decoded by an actor network to predict action distributions, which are executed in a self-play PPO loop implemented in JAX.
data_processing: Flipping all features (positions, map) to keep the player in the top-left corner
Adding x/y coordinate positional encodings to map features
Masking invalid actions (out-of-map or out-of-reach sapping)
Iterative updating of relic fragment location probabilities based on unit history
features_engineering: Relic fragment location probabilities updated iteratively from unit movement history
Positional encodings (x, y coordinates) added to map features
insights: Visualizing model inputs and outputs was essential for debugging a custom JAX implementation.
xLSTM outperformed standard LSTMs for partially observable time-series tasks while requiring fewer parameters.
The introduction of late-spawning relics made recurrent modeling highly advantageous for the competition.
Small map sizes enabled training on consumer GPUs with limited VRAM.
critical_findings: Switching to sparse rewards during training caused significantly slower model improvement compared to shaped rewards.
The model continued to improve after the final submission checkpoint, indicating that longer training would have yielded higher scores.

papers.title: JointPPO: Diving deeper into the effectiveness of PPO in multi-agent reinforcement learning
